## Current Portion of Long Term Debt | Double Entry Bookkeeping

The current portion of long term loan or long term debt is a current liability. It is the amount of debt principal repayable within 12 months of the balance sheet date. The current portion of long-term debt is not the same as short term debt.

steps.args: {'prompt': "Using Boeing's FY2024 10-K {{ba_10k_2024}}, find and quote the exact figures (in millions) for: total debt at Dec 31 2024 and its balance-sheet components; FY2024 net loss (consolidated and attributable to Boeing); loss before income taxes; income tax benefit; effective tax rate; interest and debt expense. Also state whether debt amounts are carrying values net of unamortized discounts/issuance costs. Include relevant passages/table labels."}
steps.result:
All amounts below are **in millions of dollars**.

## Debt at December 31, 2024

Boeing’s **“Consolidated Statements of Financial Position”** reports:

> “Short-term debt and current portion of long-term debt — **$1,278**”  
> “Long-term debt — **$52,586**”

Therefore:

| Balance-sheet component | Dec. 31, 2024 |
|---|---:|
| Short-term debt and current portion of long-term debt | $1,278 |
| Long-term debt | 52,586 |
| **Total debt** | **$53,864** |

This total is confirmed in **Note 16 – Debt**, whose debt table concludes:

> “Total debt — **$53,864**”

### Carrying value versus principal

**Yes.** The $53,864 total and its balance-sheet components are **carrying values**, reflecting unamortized discounts and debt-issuance costs rather than gross contractual principal.

Evidence includes:

- **Note 21 – Fair Value Measurements** explicitly reports:
  > “Debt, excluding finance lease obligations — Carrying Amount **$(53,625)**”

- Note 16 reports finance-lease obligations of **$239**. Thus:
  > $53,625 + $239 = **$53,864**

- In contrast, the MD&A contractual-obligations table reports **“Long-term debt (including current portion)”** of **$54,369**, representing contractual principal amounts. The $744 difference between $54,369 and the $53,625 carrying amount excluding finance leases reflects the net unamortized discount/issuance-cost and related carrying-value adjustments.

Boeing also states regarding its 2024 senior-note issuance:

> “The net proceeds of the issuance totaled **$9.9 billion, after deducting underwriting discounts, commissions, and offering expenses**.”

## FY2024 earnings and tax figures

From Boeing’s **“Consolidated Statements of Operations”**:

> “Interest and debt expense — **$(2,725)**”  
> “Loss before income taxes — **$(12,210)**”  
> “Income tax benefit/(expense) — **$381**”  
> “Net loss — **$(11,829)**”  
> “Less: net loss attributable to noncontrolling interest — **$(12)**”  
> “Net loss attributable to Boeing shareholders — **$(11,817)**”

Accordingly:

| FY2024 item | Amount |
|---|---:|
| Interest and debt expense | **$(2,725)** |
| Loss before income taxes | **$(12,210)** |
| Income tax benefit | **$381** |
| Consolidated net loss | **$(11,829)** |
| Net loss attributable to Boeing shareholders | **$(11,817)** |

## Effective tax rate

The **“Consolidated Results of Operations”** table reports:

> “Effective income tax rate — **3.1%**”

This is also confirmed in **Note 5 – Income Taxes**, in the table labeled **“reconciliation of the U.S. federal statutory tax to actual income tax (benefit)/expense”**:

> “Income tax (benefit)/expense — **$(381)** — **3.1%**”

The statement of operations presents the benefit as positive **$381**, while Note 5 uses parentheses because its table is labeled “tax (benefit)/expense.” The underlying amount is the same.

## Estimated impact

If all of Boeing’s debt outstanding at December 31, 2024 were refinanced at an interest rate **3 percentage points higher**, Boeing’s annual interest expense would increase by:

\[
\$53.864\text{ billion} \times 3.00\% = \$1.61592\text{ billion}
\]

Boeing reported a 2024 pretax loss of $12.210 billion and an income-tax benefit of $381 million, implying an effective tax-benefit rate of:

\[
\$381\text{ million} \div \$12{,}210\text{ million}=3.1204\%
\]

Assuming the additional interest receives the same effective tax benefit, the **after-tax reduction in net income** would be:

\[
\$1.61592\text{ billion}\times(1-3.1204\%)
=\boxed{\$1.56550\text{ billion}}
\]

Boeing’s 2024 net loss attributable to Boeing shareholders was $11.817 billion. Therefore, the incremental interest would deepen that loss by:

\[
\$1.56550\text{ billion}\div \$11.817\text{ billion}
=\boxed{13.25\%}
\]

The pro forma net loss attributable to Boeing shareholders would consequently be approximately:

\[
-\$11.817\text{ billion}-\$1.56550\text{ billion}
=\boxed{-\$13.38250\text{ billion}}
\]

### Conclusion

**Refinancing all 2024 debt at rates 3 percentage points higher would reduce Boeing’s annual net income by approximately $1.57 billion, or 13.25% relative to its reported 2024 net loss.** In other words, Boeing’s net loss would increase from $11.82 billion to approximately $13.38 billion.

### Important assumptions

- “3% higher” is interpreted as **3 percentage points higher**, not 3% multiplied by the existing rate.
- The calculation uses Boeing’s total debt carrying amount of **$53.864 billion**, consisting of $1.278 billion of short-term/current debt and $52.586 billion of long-term debt.
- It assumes the entire year-end debt balance is refinanced and remains outstanding for a full year.
- It excludes refinancing fees, premiums, changes in principal, and other transaction costs.
- It assumes Boeing can realize a tax benefit at its implied 2024 effective rate. If no additional tax benefit were realizable, net income would instead decline by the full **$1.61592 billion**, equal to **13.67%** of the reported net loss, resulting in a net loss of approximately **$13.43292 billion**.
